steps.title: Understand margin of error and sample size
steps.content: The margin of error represents the range of uncertainty in an estimate. A smaller margin of error indicates a more precise estimate. For a given population and confidence level, margin of error depends on sample size: larger samples produce smaller margins of error.

steps.title: Apply the relationship to determine which sample was larger
steps.content: Sample 1 has a margin of error of 1 minute; Sample 2 has a margin of error of 2.3 minutes. The problem states both margins were calculated the same way, meaning both use the same confidence level and method. Since both samples come from the same population (same type of oven), they estimate the same population variability. Therefore, with the same confidence level and variability, the smaller margin of error in Sample 1 must result from a larger sample size. The first sample contained more ovens than the second sample. That matches choice A — A is correct.
